content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 24, 1991


Honorable Dennis Nelson
City Councilmember
City of Yuba City
1201 Civic Center Boulevard
Yuba City, CA 95993

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-91-443

Dear Councilmember Nelson:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as a Yuba City Councilmember under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in rendering advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

     You have asked whether you may participate in Yuba City Council decisions where your employer, Laughlin and Company, is involved under the following circumstances:

     1.     A client of Laughlin and Company appears before the Yuba City Council with respect to a project on which you have worked.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

- Our File No. I-91-443
Page 4


2.  Reasonably Foreseeable Material Financial Effect

    A.  Economic Interests Directly Involved in a Decision

    Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
If there is a substantial likelihood that a city council decision
will have a financial effect on Laughlin and Company, the effect
is "foreseeable."

    The Commission has adopted a series of regulations for
determining whether the foreseeable financial effect of a decision
will be material. The standards differ depending on the nature of
the decision before the official and the economic interest
involved. (Regulation 18702.) If the economic interest is
directly involved in the decision before the official's agency,
Regulation 18702.1 provides that the effect of the decision is
deemed to be material.

    An economic interest is directly involved in a decision when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named
party to the proceeding or the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation
18702.1(b).) Regulation 18702.1 also provides that an official's
interests are directly involved in a decision and disqualification
is required where there is a nexus between the purpose for which
an official receives income and a governmental decision.

    A "nexus" exists if the official receives income to achieve a
goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or
hindered by the governmental decision. In other words, the
official may not accomplish in his public capacity what he is paid
to accomplish in his private capacity. (Regulation 18702.1(d);
Sprague Advice Letter, No. I-88-190; Chin Advice Letter, No. A-88-
091; Lopus Advice Letter, No. I-90-152.)

    According to your facts, employees of Laughlin and Company
appear before the city council in a representative capacity in
conjunction with the projects of its clients. Thus, your
questions do not involve situations where Laughlin and Company
would be considered directly involved under Regulation 18702.1(b),
because Laughlin and Company is not initiating the proceeding by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request, and is
not a named party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. Where
Laughlin and Company appears before the city council in a
representative capacity, as an agent of an unrelated business

. Our File No. I-91-443
Page 5


entity, and the property subject to the decision is the property
of the unrelated business entity, Laughlin and Company is not
directly involved in the decision before the city council. (Gill
Advice Letter, No. A-85-252.)

However, where you have worked on a project at Laughlin and
Company which then comes before the city council for
consideration, Regulation 18702.1(a) and (d) provide that Laughlin
and Company will still be deemed to be materially affected if you
would be achieving, defeating, aiding, or hindering a governmental
decision and you have received income from Laughlin and Company to
further the project. Consequently, where you have participated in
a project and this project comes before the agency, you may not
participate. (Silver Advice Letter, No. I-90-644.) This would be
true whether representatives of your employer appear before your
agency representing the project or not.

B. Economic Interests Indirectly Involved in a Decision

The other situations discussed in your letter must be
analyzed to determine if there will be an indirect material
financial effect on Laughlin and Company. As discussed above, the
clients of Laughlin and Company are not sources of income to you,
and therefore are not your economic interests.

The remaining situations include the following: (1) a client
of Laughlin and Company appears before the city council on a
project for which other employees of Laughlin and Company have
provided services, represented by Laughlin and Company; (2) a
client of Laughlin and Company appears before the city council on
a project for which other employees of Laughlin and Company have
provided services, but Laughlin and Company does not appear; (3) a
client of Laughlin and Company appears before the city council on
a project on which Laughlin and Company has not been involved.

Where Laughlin and Company is not directly before the city
council, you must still disqualify yourself if Laughlin and
Company or its owner will be indirectly materially affected by a
decision of the city council. Whether the indirect effect of a
decision on a business entity is material depends on the financial
size of Laughlin and Company. Regulation 18702.2 provides
different thresholds of materiality for the following:

1. Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange
or the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the
Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial

_______________________

4 In your letter, you indicated that Mr. Laughlin wholly owns
Laughlin and Company. As sole owner of Laughlin and Company,
Mr. Laughlin is also recognized as a source of income to you.
(Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-069.)

Our File No. I-91-443
Page 6


corporations or the 500 largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations.
(Regulation 18702.2(a) and (d).)

     2. Business entities listed on the National Association of
Securities Dealers National Market List or any business entity
with net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and pre-tax
income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000.
(Regulation 18702.2(b) and (e).)

     3. Business entities not fitting the requirements of (a) or
(b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify
for public sale in California and are listed on the Eligible
Securities List maintained by the California Department of
Corporations. Or, business entities with net tangible assets of
at least $4,000,000, and with pre-tax income for the last fiscal
year of at least $750,000, with net income from that period of at
least $400,000. (Regulation 18702.2(c) and (f).)

     4. For any business entity not covered in one of the
categories set forth above, Regulation 18702.2(g) governs.

     Assuming that Laughlin and Company is a relatively small
business entity, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that the effect of
a decision is material where:

          (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

          (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

     Thus, if subdivision (g) is the appropriate standard, you may
not participate in a city council decision that could foreseeably
increase or decrease the gross revenues, assets or liabilities of
Laughlin and Company by $10,000 or more, or increase or decrease
expenses by $2,500.

     As stated above, you are also prohibited from participating
in decisions that will indirectly affect Mr. Laughlin personally.
Where a source of income is an individual who is directly before
the city council, as in the case of Mr. Laughlin, Regulation
18702.6 provides that the indirect effect of a decision is

Our File No. I-91-443
Page 7


material as to the source of income if the decision will affect
his income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities by $1,000 or more.5

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. If you have
any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


5 If Mr. Laughlin's property in involved in a decision, the
effect of the decisions is material if the decision will affect
the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.

6 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
You have asked whether you may participate in Yuba City Council decisions where your employer, Laughlin and Company, is involved under the following circumstances:

1. A client of Laughlin and Company appears before the Yuba City Council with respect to a project on which you have worked.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

2. Reasonably Foreseeable Material Financial Effect

A. Economic Interests Directly Involved in a Decision

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) If there is a substantial likelihood that a city council decision will have a financial effect on Laughlin and Company, the effect is "foreseeable."

The Commission has adopted a series of regulations for determining whether the foreseeable financial effect of a decision will be material. The standards differ depending on the nature of the decision before the official and the economic interest involved. (Regulation 18702.) If the economic interest is directly involved in the decision before the official's agency, Regulation 18702.1 provides that the effect of the decision is deemed to be material.

An economic interest is directly involved in a decision when the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18702.1(b).) Regulation 18702.1 also provides that an official's interests are directly involved in a decision and disqualification is required where there is a nexus between the purpose for which an official receives income and a governmental decision.

A "nexus" exists if the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the governmental decision. In other words, the official may not accomplish in his public capacity what he is paid to accomplish in his private capacity. (Regulation 18702.1(d); Sprague Advice Letter, No. I-88-190; Chin Advice Letter, No. A-88-091; Lopus Advice Letter, No. I-90-152.)

According to your facts, employees of Laughlin and Company appear before the city council in a representative capacity in conjunction with the projects of its clients. Thus, your questions do not involve situations where Laughlin and Company would be considered directly involved under Regulation 18702.1(b), because Laughlin and Company is not initiating the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request, and is not a named party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. Where Laughlin and Company appears before the city council in a representative capacity, as an agent of an unrelated business

entity, and the property subject to the decision is the property of the unrelated business entity, Laughlin and Company is not directly involved in the decision before the city council. (Gill Advice Letter, No. A-85-252.)

However, where you have worked on a project at Laughlin and Company which then comes before the city council for consideration, Regulation 18702.1(a) and (d) provide that Laughlin and Company will still be deemed to be materially affected if you would be achieving, defeating, aiding, or hindering a governmental decision and you have received income from Laughlin and Company to further the project. Consequently, where you have participated in a project and this project comes before the agency, you may not participate. (Silver Advice Letter, No. I-90-644.) This would be true whether representatives of your employer appear before your agency representing the project or not.

B. Economic Interests Indirectly Involved in a Decision

The other situations discussed in your letter must be analyzed to determine if there will be an indirect material financial effect on Laughlin and Company. As discussed above, the clients of Laughlin and Company are not sources of income to you, and therefore are not your economic interests.

The remaining situations include the following: (1) a client of Laughlin and Company appears before the city council on a project for which other employees of Laughlin and Company have provided services, represented by Laughlin and Company; (2) a client of Laughlin and Company appears before the city council on a project for which other employees of Laughlin and Company have provided services, but Laughlin and Company does not appear; (3) a client of Laughlin and Company appears before the city council on a project on which Laughlin and Company has not been involved.

Where Laughlin and Company is not directly before the city council, you must still disqualify yourself if Laughlin and Company or its owner will be indirectly materially affected by a decision of the city council.4 Whether the indirect effect of a decision on a business entity is material depends on the financial size of Laughlin and Company. Regulation 18702.2 provides different thresholds of materiality for the following:

1. Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange or the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial

4 In your letter, you indicated that Mr. Laughlin wholly owns Laughlin and Company. As sole owner of Laughlin and Company, Mr. Laughlin is also recognized as a source of income to you. (Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-069.)

corporations or the 500 largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations. (Regulation 18702.2(a) and (d).)

2. Business entities listed on the National Association of Securities Dealers National Market List or any business entity with net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000. (Regulation 18702.2(b) and (e).)

3. Business entities not fitting the requirements of (a) or (b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify for public sale in California and are listed on the Eligible Securities List maintained by the California Department of Corporations. Or, business entities with net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000, and with pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at least $750,000, with net income from that period of at least $400,000. (Regulation 18702.2(c) and (f).)

4. For any business entity not covered in one of the categories set forth above, Regulation 18702.2(g) governs.

Assuming that Laughlin and Company is a relatively small business entity, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that the effect of a decision is material where:

(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in the increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.

Thus, if subdivision (g) is the appropriate standard, you may not participate in a city council decision that could foreseeably increase or decrease the gross revenues, assets or liabilities of Laughlin and Company by $10,000 or more, or increase or decrease expenses by $2,500.

As stated above, you are also prohibited from participating in decisions that will indirectly affect Mr. Laughlin personally. Where a source of income is an individual who is directly before the city council, as in the case of Mr. Laughlin, Regulation 18702.6 provides that the indirect effect of a decision is

material as to the source of income if the decision will affect his income, investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities by $1,000 or more.5

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6
